meta.title: Personality Module - Master Item Bank
meta.scoring: Reverse-key items where reverse_score=true using 6 - x, then mean per trait, convert to percentile vs. norm sample, then T-score (M=50, SD=10).
meta.provenance_note: IPIP / IPIP-HEXACO items are public domain; verify exact canonical wording at ipip.ori.org before production. Items tagged 'IPIP-style original' were authored for this project and are license-free to the project owner. WLCS-style and DOSPERT-style items follow those open instruments and require citation. Dark Triad scales (SD3, Dirty Dozen) and several others (Duckworth Grit, original NCS, TEIQue, Schwarzer GSE, Bateman-Crant Proactive) are copyrighted and are NOT reproduced.
meta.legal_note: Dark Triad traits (Machiavellianism, Narcissism, Psychopathy) and very low Honesty-Humility must be used as human-review FLAGS, never as automatic disqualifiers. See the specification's Legal & Ethical section (EU AI Act Annex III high-risk; GDPR Art. 22; Norwegian Working Environment Act s.9-3).

Conscientiousness.definition: Tendency to be organized, dependable, disciplined, and achievement-oriented.
Conscientiousness.validity_summary: Strongest, most generalizable Big Five predictor of overall job performance (corrected rho ~.20-.22; Barrick & Mount 1991; Hurtz & Donovan 2000). Validity highest in autonomous/unsupervised roles.

items.text: I get chores and tasks done right away.
I like order and keep things tidy.
I follow a schedule and stick to plans.
I am exacting and thorough in my work.
I pay close attention to details.
I leave my belongings lying around.
I make a mess of things.
I often forget to put things back where they belong.
I shirk or postpone my duties.
I waste my time instead of getting to work.

Extraversion.definition: Sociability, assertiveness, energy, and positive emotionality.
Extraversion.validity_summary: Predicts performance in sales and management roles (Barrick & Mount 1991; Wilmot & Ones 2021); rho ~.31 with leadership emergence (Judge et al. 2002).

items.text: I feel comfortable around people.
I start conversations easily.
I talk to many different people at social events.
I don't mind being the center of attention.
I feel energized after spending time with others.
I don't talk a lot.
I keep in the background.
I have little to say.
I prefer not to draw attention to myself.
I am quiet around strangers.

Emotional Stability.framework: Five-Factor Model (Big Five, reverse of Neuroticism)
Emotional Stability.definition: Calmness, resilience, and freedom from anxiety, anger, and mood volatility. Scored so that HIGHER = more stable.
Emotional Stability.validity_summary: Second most generalizable Big Five predictor; matters most in high-stress, safety-critical, and skilled/semiskilled roles (Hurtz & Donovan 2000; Wilmot & Ones 2021). Keep items behavioral, not clinical.

items.framework: Five-Factor Model (Big Five, reverse of Neuroticism)
items.text: I stay relaxed most of the time.

items.framework: Five-Factor Model (Big Five, reverse of Neuroticism)
items.text: I seldom feel down or blue.

items.framework: Five-Factor Model (Big Five, reverse of Neuroticism)
items.text: I rarely get irritated.

items.framework: Five-Factor Model (Big Five, reverse of Neuroticism)
items.text: I remain calm under pressure.

items.framework: Five-Factor Model (Big Five, reverse of Neuroticism)
items.text: I am not easily bothered by setbacks.

items.framework: Five-Factor Model (Big Five, reverse of Neuroticism)
items.text: I get stressed out easily.

items.framework: Five-Factor Model (Big Five, reverse of Neuroticism)
items.text: I worry about things a lot.

items.framework: Five-Factor Model (Big Five, reverse of Neuroticism)
items.text: I am easily disturbed by events around me.

items.framework: Five-Factor Model (Big Five, reverse of Neuroticism)
items.text: I get upset easily.

items.framework: Five-Factor Model (Big Five, reverse of Neuroticism)
items.text: My mood changes frequently.

Openness to Experience.definition: Intellectual curiosity, imagination, and preference for novelty and variety.
Openness to Experience.validity_summary: Predicts training proficiency and performance in creative/professional roles (Barrick & Mount 1991; Wilmot & Ones 2021).

items.text: I have a vivid imagination.
I come up with good, original ideas.
I enjoy thinking about complex, abstract problems.
I am full of new ideas.
I seek out new experiences and perspectives.
I am not interested in abstract ideas.
I do not have a good imagination.
I have difficulty understanding abstract concepts.
I avoid difficult or challenging material.
I prefer familiar routines to new experiences.

Agreeableness.definition: Compassion, cooperation, trust, and consideration for others.
Agreeableness.validity_summary: Matters in teamwork- and service-heavy roles; relates to OCB (Pletzer et al. 2021). Very high levels can hinder firm negotiation/feedback roles.

items.text: I sympathize with others' feelings.
I take time out for other people.
I sense and respond to others' emotions.
I make people feel at ease.
I am genuinely interested in other people.
I am not really interested in others.
I sometimes insult people.
I am not interested in other people's problems.
I feel little concern for others.
I can have a sharp tongue with people.

Honesty-Humility.definition: Sincerity, fairness, greed-avoidance, and modesty (the 6th HEXACO factor).
Honesty-Humility.validity_summary: Strongest single personality predictor of (low) Counterproductive Work Behavior, with incremental validity over the Big Five, GMA, and integrity tests (Lee, Berry & Gonzalez-Mule 2019; Pletzer et al. 2019).

items.text: I wouldn't use flattery to get a raise or promotion, even if I thought it would work.
If I knew I could never get caught, I would still refuse to steal a large sum of money.
Having a lot of money is not especially important to me.
I am an ordinary person who is no better than others.
I wouldn't pretend to like someone just to get favors from them.
If I were sure I could get away with it, I would be willing to bend the rules for personal gain.
I want people to know that I am an important person of high status.
I think I am entitled to more respect than the average person.
I would like to live in a very expensive, high-status neighborhood.
I would be tempted to take a shortcut that benefited me at someone else's expense.

Machiavellianism.source: IPIP-style original (maps to manipulation / low Honesty-Humility)
Machiavellianism.definition: Cynical, strategic manipulation of others for self-interest.
Machiavellianism.validity_summary: Negatively related to performance and positively to CWB (O'Boyle et al. 2012). LEGAL NOTE: use as a human-review FLAG, never an automatic disqualifier. Prefer inference via low Honesty-Humility.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to manipulation / low Honesty-Humility)
items.text: I find it useful to manage situations behind the scenes to get what I want.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to manipulation / low Honesty-Humility)
items.text: It is wise to keep track of information you might use later.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to manipulation / low Honesty-Humility)
items.text: I am willing to use flattery to get my way.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to manipulation / low Honesty-Humility)
items.text: I prefer to influence people indirectly rather than confront them.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to manipulation / low Honesty-Humility)
items.text: People who are completely open about their intentions put themselves at a disadvantage.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to manipulation / low Honesty-Humility)
items.text: I prefer to be straightforward and transparent in my dealings.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to manipulation / low Honesty-Humility)
items.text: I believe most people can be trusted to act in good faith.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to manipulation / low Honesty-Humility)
items.text: Manipulating others to reach a goal feels wrong to me.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to manipulation / low Honesty-Humility)
items.text: I rarely think about the hidden motives behind what people say.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to manipulation / low Honesty-Humility)
items.text: I would rather give up an advantage than mislead someone.

Narcissism.source: IPIP-style original (maps to grandiosity; NPI is copyrighted)
Narcissism.definition: Grandiosity, entitlement, and a need for admiration.
Narcissism.validity_summary: Mixed effects on performance; linked to CWB and risky leadership (O'Boyle et al. 2012). LEGAL NOTE: human-review FLAG only.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to grandiosity; NPI is copyrighted)
items.text: I deserve special recognition for my contributions.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to grandiosity; NPI is copyrighted)
items.text: I am more capable than most of the people around me.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to grandiosity; NPI is copyrighted)
items.text: I enjoy being the center of attention.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to grandiosity; NPI is copyrighted)
items.text: People should acknowledge how talented I am.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to grandiosity; NPI is copyrighted)
items.text: I expect a great deal from the people I work with.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to grandiosity; NPI is copyrighted)
items.text: I am no more deserving of praise than my colleagues.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to grandiosity; NPI is copyrighted)
items.text: I am uncomfortable when people single me out for attention.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to grandiosity; NPI is copyrighted)
items.text: I think of myself as an ordinary member of the team.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to grandiosity; NPI is copyrighted)
items.text: I rarely feel that I am better than others.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to grandiosity; NPI is copyrighted)
items.text: I am happy to let other people take the credit.

Psychopathy.source: IPIP-style original (maps to callous affect / impulsivity)
Psychopathy.definition: Callous affect, low empathy, and impulsive disregard for others.
Psychopathy.validity_summary: Most consistently negative Dark-Triad correlate of performance and strongest CWB link (O'Boyle et al. 2012). HIGH LEGAL SENSITIVITY: human-review FLAG only; avoid clinical labels in candidate-facing text.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to callous affect / impulsivity)
items.text: I am not very concerned about how my actions affect other people.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to callous affect / impulsivity)
items.text: I tend to act on impulse without weighing the consequences.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to callous affect / impulsivity)
items.text: I rarely feel guilty after upsetting someone.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to callous affect / impulsivity)
items.text: Other people's distress does not affect me much.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to callous affect / impulsivity)
items.text: Rules are made to be broken when they get in my way.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to callous affect / impulsivity)
items.text: I feel genuine remorse when I let people down.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to callous affect / impulsivity)
items.text: I am very sensitive to other people's suffering.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to callous affect / impulsivity)
items.text: I think carefully before I act.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to callous affect / impulsivity)
items.text: I would feel bad for a long time if I hurt someone.

items.source: IPIP-style original (maps to callous affect / impulsivity)
items.text: I try hard to follow the rules even when no one is watching.

Locus of Control (Internal).definition: Belief that work outcomes follow from one's own effort and ability (internal) rather than luck or powerful others (external). Scored so HIGHER = more internal.
Locus of Control (Internal).validity_summary: Internal LOC ~rho .22 with job performance and a Core Self-Evaluations component (Judge & Bono 2001; Ng, Sorensen & Eby 2006).

items.text: On most jobs, people can pretty much accomplish whatever they set out to accomplish.
If you know what you want from a job, you can find a job that gives it to you.
Promotions are generally given to employees who perform well.
People who perform their jobs well usually get rewarded.
My own effort is the main thing that determines my success at work.
Getting the job you want is mostly a matter of luck.
Making money at work is primarily a matter of good fortune.
Promotions are usually a matter of being lucky.
Who you know matters more than how well you do your work.
It takes a lot of luck to be an outstanding employee.

Grit (Perseverance of Effort).definition: Sustained effort and persistence toward long-term goals. Only the perseverance-of-effort facet is modelled (the facet that carries the criterion validity).
Grit (Perseverance of Effort).validity_summary: Grit overlaps almost entirely with Conscientiousness (rho ~.84; Crede, Tynan & Harms 2017). Perseverance-of-effort facet predicts; consistency-of-interests facet does not. Treat as a Conscientiousness sub-facet for incremental-validity purposes.

items.text: I finish whatever I begin.
I am a hard worker.
I am diligent and persistent.
Setbacks don't discourage me; I bounce back and keep going.
I carry my plans through to completion.
I have a hard time finishing tasks that take a long time.
I give up easily when things get difficult.
I often lose focus before completing a project.
Obstacles tend to discourage me.
I abandon goals that turn out to be harder than expected.

Emotional Intelligence (Trait).definition: Self-reported ability to perceive, understand, and regulate emotions in oneself and others.
Emotional Intelligence (Trait).validity_summary: Mixed/trait EI shows rho ~.47 with performance but is heavily confounded with Conscientiousness and (low) Neuroticism (Joseph & Newman 2010). Most useful as a moderator for high-emotional-labour roles.

items.text: I am good at reading other people's emotions.
I understand why my own emotions change.
I can stay composed and manage my feelings in tense situations.
I notice how others are feeling before they say anything.
I can usually help calm someone who is upset.
I find it hard to tell what others are feeling.
I am often confused about which emotion I am feeling.
I lose control of my emotions when I am stressed.
I rarely notice the emotional reactions of people around me.
I struggle to help others manage their feelings.

Tolerance for Ambiguity.definition: Comfort acting and deciding under incomplete, novel, or unstructured conditions.
Tolerance for Ambiguity.validity_summary: Relates to adaptability and innovation (Furnham & Ribchester 1995; Herman et al. 2010); domain-specific r ~.15-.25.

items.text: I am comfortable making decisions with incomplete information.
I enjoy situations where there is no clear right answer.
I can work effectively even when goals are unclear.
I adapt easily when plans change unexpectedly.
I find uncertainty interesting rather than threatening.
I feel uneasy when a situation is unpredictable.
I strongly prefer tasks with clear, detailed instructions.
I get frustrated when a problem has no definite solution.
I dislike starting work before everything is clearly defined.
I need certainty before I can move forward.

Need for Achievement.definition: Drive to meet high standards and accomplish challenging goals.
Need for Achievement.validity_summary: Correlates ~.20-.30 with entrepreneurial and sales performance (McClelland; Spangler 1992).

items.text: I set high standards for myself and for others.
I work hard to be among the best at what I do.
I go straight for the goal.
I am driven to accomplish ambitious targets.
I consistently do more than what is expected of me.
I am satisfied with simply getting the job done adequately.
I do just enough work to get by.
I put little effort into reaching difficult goals.
I am content to stay at my current level of performance.
I avoid taking on challenging targets.

Need for Cognition.definition: Tendency to engage in and enjoy effortful, complex thinking.
Need for Cognition.validity_summary: Predicts learning and complex problem solving; stronger links in cognitively complex roles (Cacioppo & Petty 1982). The original NCS is copyrighted, so IPIP Intellect is used.

items.text: I enjoy thinking through complex problems.
I prefer tasks that challenge me intellectually.
I like to solve puzzles and figure things out.
I would rather analyze a problem deeply than move on quickly.
I enjoy learning new and difficult material.
I avoid situations that require a lot of thinking.
I prefer simple problems to complex ones.
Thinking hard for long periods tires me out.
I only think as hard as I have to.
I find little satisfaction in deliberating over difficult issues.

Generalized Self-Efficacy.definition: Baseline confidence in one's ability to handle novel and difficult tasks.
Generalized Self-Efficacy.validity_summary: rho ~.23 with job performance; a Core Self-Evaluations component (Judge & Bono 2001).

items.text: I am confident I can handle most problems that come my way.
If I try hard enough, I can usually solve difficult situations.
I complete tasks successfully.
I believe in my ability to learn what a new role requires.
I trust myself to perform well under pressure.
I doubt my ability to deal with unexpected problems.
I often feel I am not capable of handling difficult tasks.
I give up on tasks when I'm unsure I can complete them.
I feel out of my depth when facing new challenges.
I find it hard to believe I can reach my goals.

Proactive Personality.definition: Disposition to identify opportunities, take initiative, and change one's environment.
Proactive Personality.validity_summary: Incremental validity over the Big Five for performance and career success (Thomas, Whitman & Viswesvaran 2010). Bateman-Crant scale is copyrighted, so IPIP facets + originals are used.

items.text: I actively look for ways to improve how things are done.
If I see something I don't like, I work to change it.
I take initiative without waiting to be told.
I create opportunities rather than waiting for them.
I am constantly looking for new ways to make things better.
I wait for others to take the lead before I act.
I rarely speak up to suggest improvements.
I prefer to stick with how things are currently done.
I let others identify the problems that need solving.
I avoid taking on responsibilities that aren't assigned to me.

Risk Tolerance.definition: Comfort making high-variance, high-stakes decisions under potential downside.
Risk Tolerance.validity_summary: Domain-specific; financial and career-risk domains relate most to entrepreneurial and finance roles (Weber, Blais & Betz 2002).

items.text: I am willing to take significant risks to achieve a large reward.
I would back a venture with high potential return despite high uncertainty.
I am comfortable making bold decisions involving money.
I would choose a role with an uncertain but potentially high payoff over a stable one.
I enjoy taking chances.
I prefer safe choices with predictable outcomes.
I avoid decisions where I could lose a lot.
I get anxious when outcomes are uncertain.
I would rather have a guaranteed modest result than gamble for more.
I steer clear of high-stakes situations.

Future Time Perspective.source: IPIP-style original (ZTPI is copyrighted for commercial use)
Future Time Perspective.definition: Degree to which a person prioritizes long-term outcomes over immediate rewards.
Future Time Perspective.validity_summary: Future orientation modestly predicts planning, persistence, and delay-of-gratification behaviours (Zimbardo & Boyd 1999; Carstensen).

items.source: IPIP-style original (ZTPI is copyrighted for commercial use)
items.text: I make plans with long-term goals in mind.

items.source: IPIP-style original (ZTPI is copyrighted for commercial use)
items.text: I am willing to give up short-term rewards for long-term gains.

items.source: IPIP-style original (ZTPI is copyrighted for commercial use)
items.text: I think a lot about how today's choices affect my future.

items.source: IPIP-style original (ZTPI is copyrighted for commercial use)
items.text: I set goals years in advance.

items.source: IPIP-style original (ZTPI is copyrighted for commercial use)
items.text: I prioritize building for the future over immediate enjoyment.

items.source: IPIP-style original (ZTPI is copyrighted for commercial use)
items.text: I focus mostly on what I can get right now.

items.source: IPIP-style original (ZTPI is copyrighted for commercial use)
items.text: I rarely think about the long-term consequences of my actions.

items.source: IPIP-style original (ZTPI is copyrighted for commercial use)
items.text: I prefer immediate rewards over future ones.

items.source: IPIP-style original (ZTPI is copyrighted for commercial use)
items.text: I find it hard to stay motivated by distant goals.

items.source: IPIP-style original (ZTPI is copyrighted for commercial use)
items.text: I live mostly in the present without much thought for later.
